coredata.dc:title: Memory effect in carbon quantum DOT–PEG1500N composites
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mihalache, Iulia
Veca, L. Monica
Kusko, Mihaela
Dragoman, Daniela
coredata.dc:description: Electrical measurements performed on films of bare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as well as of CQD functionalized with PEG1500N deposited on interdigitated electrodes reveal a significant resistance hysteresis. At room temperature, both systems present a stable memory effect after a large number of cycles, fact associated with the removal of shallow trapping states after the thermal treatment of bare and passivated CQDs films. Temperature analysis of transport properties shows that CQDs functionalized with PEG1500N present a stable hysteresis as the temperature increases, in contrast to the hysteresis of bare CQD films, which disappears at high temperatures. Several transport mechanisms responsible for the electrical behavior of these systems were analyzed and the role of PEG1500N as a passivation shell was discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Electrical hysteresis
Memory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sample preparation and test device fabrication
Memory effects in carbon quantum dots
Memory effects in carbon quantum dots–PEG1500N composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the process steps used to synthesize both bare CQD and CQD–PEG1500N.
HR-TEM image of the CQD–PEG1500N composite.
SEM image of the CQD–PEG1500N film deposited on the surface of the electrode with the inset of the ID electrode pattern.

I–V characteristics of bare CQDs film deposited on ID electrodes (a) at a fix scan rate of 0.5 V/s for up to 40 cycles, and (b) at different scan rates, from 0.001 V/s to 1 V/s. Inset of Fig. 4(a): I–V characteristics in air and in vacuum.
(a) Transient charging and discharging currents obtained for a bare CQD film polarized at the constant bias of 5 V for a 100 s and subsequently unpolarized. (b) Retention characteristics.

(a) I–V characteristics of bare CQD film at different temperatures, up to 132 °C; inset: Temperature dependence of the current measured at 5 V bias. (b) Fitting according to the thermally activated hopping mechanism; inset graph shows a plot of logI against logV at positive voltage forward sweep.

I–V characteristics of CQD–PEG1500N nanocomposite film deposited on ID electrodes: (a) at a fix scan rat of 0.5 V/s for up to 10 cycles, and (b) at different scan rates, from 0.001 V/s to 1 V/s; inset: I–V characteristics at different sweeping voltage ranges.
Current–voltage characteristics after different durations of thermal treatment.
(a) Transient charging and discharging currents obtained for a CQDs–PEG1500N film polarized at the constant bias of 5 V for 100 s and subsequently depolarized. (b) Retention characteristics.

(a) I–V characteristics of CQD–PEG1500N nanocomposite film at different temperatures, up to 132 °C; left inset: I–V characteristics below room temperature, up to −30 °C; right inset: Temperature dependence of the current measured at 5 V bias. (b) Fitting with a thermionic emission mechanism.
Relaxation times and stored charge in bare CQDs for different bias pulses.
Relaxation times and stored charge in CQD–PEG1500N composites for different bias pulses.
Relaxation times in CQD–PEG1500N composites for different temperatures, at an applied step voltage of 5 V.

head.ce:title: Memory effect in carbon quantum DOT–PEG1500N composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Research and Development Institute in Microtechnologies, Str. Erou Iancu Nicolae 126 A, 077190 Bucharest, Romania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Research and Development Institute in Microtechnologies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Univ. Bucharest, Faculty of Physics, P.O. Box MG-11, 077125 Bucharest, Romania

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrical measurements performed on films of bare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as well as of CQD functionalized with PEG1500N deposited on interdigitated electrodes reveal a significant resistance hysteresis. At room temperature, both systems present a stable memory effect after a large number of cycles, fact associated with the removal of shallow trapping states after the thermal treatment of bare and passivated CQDs films. Temperature analysis of transport properties shows that CQDs functionalized with PEG1500N present a stable hysteresis as the temperature increases, in contrast to the hysteresis of bare CQD films, which disappears at high temperatures. Several transport mechanisms responsible for the electrical behavior of these systems were analyzed and the role of PEG1500N as a passivation shell was discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Memory devices such as memristors [1], memcapacitors or meminductors [2], with resistance, capacitance and, respectively, inductance states dependent on the history of the system, are increasingly sought after for computing and communication applications. In particular, memristors (or memory resistors) have been identified in inorganic structures such as TiO2 [3] and VO2 [4] thin films, where they were associated to current-activated motion of oxygen vacancies and, respectively, to the metal-to-insulator transition. Heterostructures of such resistive switching devices allow a better control of current–voltage characteristics, which can be optimized for a wide range of memory and logic applications [5]. Organic materials or hybrid structures also show memory effects due to several physical mechanisms. For instance, stable negative differential resistance regions due to changes in the properties of molecule–electrode interfaces [6,7] and/or bistable behavior useful for non-volatile memory elements [8] have been demonstrated in these materials.

Above all, memory effects occur naturally in nanoscale systems, in which a bias voltage couples solid-state ionic and electronic transport [3]. Indeed, switching between low- and high-conductance states have been evidenced in nanowire-based inorganic devices [9] and many types of metallic or non-metallic nanoparticles in organic hosts [8], including carbon-based materials (e.g. fullerene C60 [10], carbon nanotubes [11], graphene oxide [12]).

Colloidal quantum dots in general [13] and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in particular [14], are versatile systems, relatively easy to obtain with tunable diameters, and possess unique photophysical properties. Owing to their optical properties, low production costs and lack of toxicity, several studies have ascertained their viability in optical imaging and biomedical applications [15]. There is a continuous interest on better understanding and improving the optical properties of CQDs, in particular their photoluminescence, but there are scarce studies on the electrical properties of CQD-based systems.

In this context, the focus of our study is to investigate the hysteresis and memory effects of both bare CQD and CQD–PEG1500N composites (CQD functionalized with PEG1500N). Preliminary research on memory effects in CQD systems showed that the current as well as the resistance hysteresis decrease after few tens of cycles, charge trapping on shallow and deep levels being responsible for these effects [16]. On the contrary, we show that reproducible resistance hysteresis after tens of cycles in both bare CQDs and CQD–PEG1500N composites can be obtained by thermal treatment of these systems. The persistence of resistance hysteresis after CQD functionalization (in our case with PEG1500N) could be used for devices with enhanced functionality: memory devices and/or sensors of different substances. Moreover, unlike in bare CQDs, in CQD–PEG1500N composites the resistance hysteresis is preserved even at high temperatures. The conduction mechanisms in bare and functionalized CQDs are also investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation and test device fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: Electrical characterizations of CQD and CQD–PEG1500N films were carried out using a two-probe interdigitated (ID) array electrode configuration. The ID electrodes are fabricated on insulating glass substrates, have an active area of 0.04 mm2, and consist of 25 fingers of 4 μm width and 200 μm length. These ID electrodes, with gap spacing of 3 μm, were obtained using a typical photolithography process and metal evaporation. The electrode includes an adhesion layer of Cr, 20 nm thick, and a 200-nm-thick overlayer of gold.

CQDs films were prepared by drop casting the colloidal solution on the ID structure, and were dried under vacuum at 100 °C for up to 3 days prior to electrical measurements, thus removing any trapped solvent. A SEM image of the CQD–PEG1500N film deposited on an ID electrode identical to that used in our experiments but configured on a 70 nm thick SiO2 substrate is shown in Fig. 3 ; it reveals a film thickness of 1.3 μm. The inset of Fig. 3 represents a top view SEM image of the ID electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Memory effects in carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: We performed first room-temperature current–voltage measurements of ID electrodes covered with bare CQDs at a scan rate of 0.5 V/s. (This scan rate is used in all other measurements, unless specified otherwise.) We observed that the memory effect is stable after a significant number of cycles (Fig. 4 (a)), and under different sweeping rates (Fig. 4(b)). The current measured when the voltage is swept from negative to positive values is higher than the one measured for the opposite sweeping direction. The area of the hysteresis loop increases as the voltage bias range increases due to stronger detrapping of localized charges at higher voltages/applied electric fields [18]. A confirmation of the origin of the hysteretic effect as due to charge trapping in adsorbates is the disappearance of the resistance hysteresis and the significant decrease of the current as the samples are placed in vacuum, as shown in the inset of Fig. 4(a) (see also [16]). The structures, re-measured periodically for two months, preserved almost unchanged the original hysteretic effect, which demonstrates that CQD-based memory devices can have a long-term stability.

However, the thermal treatment applied to our CQD-based films enhances significantly their stability after a considerable number of cycles: after 20 cycles the hysteresis curve is practically the same, whereas similar studies reported a reduced hysteresis after the same number of cycles [16]. Because the resistance hysteresis is caused by charge trapping states associated to surface adsorbates/dopants [11,12,16], an increase in stability after annealing can be associated to the removal of the shallow charge traps, process known as thermal healing. Thermal healing at temperatures of some hundreds degrees Celsius has been demonstrated to dehydrogenate graphene sheets [19]. In our case, thermal healing at lower temperatures is favored by the large number of edge states in CQDs.

Transient currents obtained when the sample was polarized at the constant bias of 5V for 100 s and subsequently unpolarized are presented in Fig. 5 (a) (inset: time dependence of the voltage pulse), being very well approximated with a biexponential function I = I 0 + I 1 exp(−t/τ 1) + I 2 exp(−t/τ 2), which shows that room-temperature charge transport is dominated by two trapping states. This behavior is consistent with charge injection into CQD and the following filling of the charge trap sites [11,16]. The relaxation times τ 1 and τ 2 are somewhat large due to the removal of the (very) shallow trap states after the thermal treatment applied during the drying process of CQDs, which leads also to an improved stability after several cycles.

To gain some information about the nature of trap sites, we calculated the relaxation times from transient currents at different applied step voltages. The results in Table 1 show that τ 1 and τ 2 depend on the applied step voltage at both charging and discharging processes and, moreover, that the dependence on the step voltage is non-uniform. We assume that this behavior is due to the coexistence of numerous trapping states, different states becoming dominant at different step voltages. Carrier redistribution among the charge trapping states, especially among the shallow ones occurs in a very short time (the hysteresis curve stabilizes at sweeping rates faster than 0.5 V/s). The stored charge is computed by integrating the discharging current over time and has a non-uniform behavior with the step voltage, as can be seen from Table 1.

The retention characteristic at room temperature, measured as in [20], is represented in Fig. 5(b); the sequence of applied pulses is shown in the inset. Similar to [21], the memory window narrows rapidly in an initial stage, most probable due to depleting of the shallowest charge trapping states, and then narrows much more slowly, the high- and low-resistance states being distinguishable up to 300 s. As a result, the on/off current ratio decreases in time and retains a value larger than, say, 1.5 for up to 45 s. As such, CQD-based films, even in this non-optimized configuration, could be used as DRAM, the standard retention time of this type of memories being as low as 64ms [22].

For memory devices working at room temperature, it is important that the hysteresis effect is maintained at and above this temperature. To study the robustness of the resistance hysteresis against increasing temperature, we plotted I–V characteristics above room temperature, up to 132 °C (see Fig. 6(a)). Our results show that the current at a constant voltage V = 5 V increases with temperature, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 6(a), whereas the hysteresis loop decreases significantly and practically disappears for high temperatures. This behavior suggests that as the temperature increases, a non-hysteretic thermally activated hopping mechanism becomes dominant [23], the current having in this case the dependence I∝Vexp(−E
a /k
B
T) with E
a the activation energy and k
B the Boltzmann constant. This assumption is verified by the representation in Fig. 6(b), as well as by the observation that the resistance is independent on the applied voltage (the slope of the curve in the inset of Fig. 6(b) is practically 1). Note that the activation energies obtained from Fig. 6(b) have two distinct values (in agreement with the two dominant relaxation times): 0.26 eV at low temperatures and 0.43 eV at high temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Memory effects in carbon quantum dots–PEG1500N composites
ce:section.ce:para: Another goal of our study was to investigate if the memory effect in bare CQDs is maintained after functionalization with PEG1500N and if functionalization enhances the thermal stability of the resistance hysteresis. Because the diameters of the crystalline CQDs are smaller than the 10 nm-long polymeric chain that wraps around it, the nanocomposite can be seen as a core–shell structure. The composite is dropcasted and dried in identical conditions as for bare CQDs prior to electrical characterization.

Our measurements reveal that the hysteretic behavior is preserved after CQD functionalization. As illustrated in Fig. 7 (a), the resistance hysteresis is still stable at a large number of cycles, and increases with increasing sweeping rate, as shown in Fig. 7(b), confirming that traps able to charge and discharge very fast are involved also in current transport in CQD–PEG1500N composites. As for bare CQDs, the area of the hysteresis loop increases as the voltage range increases, as can be seen from the inset of Fig. 7(b).

The magnitude of the resistance hysteresis depends, however, on the duration of the thermal treatment at 100 °C. From Fig. 8 it can be seen that negative differential resistance regions can be observed after a thermal treatment of only 4 h, associated to remains of the solvent, which is not completely removed. For longer thermal treatments (after 7 and 14 h) the magnitude of the hysteresis increases, which indicates that residual atoms adsorb progressively on the surface of the CQD-composite as the solvent dries. For still longer thermal treatments desorbtion processes remove the weakest attached adsorbates and the resistance increases.

The transient currents after applying several step voltages (see Fig. 9 (a)) can be excellently approximated with a biexponential expression, which demonstrates that, again, only two trapping states are determinant in charge transport. The dependences on the applied step voltage of the relaxation times τ 1 and τ 2 at charging and discharging, as well as of the charge stored in the structure, Q, are presented in Table 2 . All step voltage pulses were of 100 s duration. The relaxation times in the CQD–PEG1500N composites are faster than in bare CQDs, suggesting that the PEG1500N polymer modifies the charge trapping levels.

The differences with respect to the bare CQD of the CQD–PEG1500N composite, which can be regarded as a core–shell nanoparticle, are: (i) the density of trapping states at the interface between CQD and PEG1500N are modified, and (ii) the trapping states associated with surface adsorbates are now related to PEG1500N and not to CQDs. In fact, surface passivation of nanostructures has been shown to reduce the interface trap densities and to modify the densities of fixed charges [24], effects that reduce drastically the 1/f noise. The uniform increase of the charging times and the stored charge with the step voltage suggests that the charge redistribution processes among various trapping states are significantly reduced. Two trapping states (or two distributions of such states with close energy levels), shallow and deep, respectively, contribute dominantly to charge transport. Because at thermal treatments of about 3 days we estimate that the number of adsorbed molecules on the composite surface is relatively small, we suggest that the resistance hysteresis can still be associated to CQDs, which are more “protected” from the environment due to the PEG1500N coating layer. Indeed, in efficient passivation processes, such as PEG1500N passivation of CQDs [25], quantum dots are surrounded by layers with a higher bandgap [26,27], which confine the charge carriers.

A more efficient charge carrier confinement leads to an increase retention time. Indeed, in CQD–PEG1500N composites at room temperature the high- and low-resistance states can be distinguished for times up to about 800 s, as can be seen from Fig. 9(b). As in bare CQDs, the hysteresis window and the on/off ratio decrease rapidly initially, and slower afterward, the last parameter acquiring values larger than 1.5 for up to 50 s. Use of the CQD–PEG1500N composites as DRAM is, therefore, possible.

Because the stability of the resistance hysteresis at and above room temperature is essential for devices working in ambient conditions, we have measured current–voltage dependences at different temperatures for the CQD–PEG1500N composite. The results, displayed in Fig. 10 (a), show that, unlike for bare CQDs, the hysteresis loop is maintained at high temperatures. On the contrary, below room temperature the current decreases significantly, as shown in the left inset of Fig. 10(a). Functionalized/passivated CQDs can thus act as memory devices at much larger temperatures than bare CQDs. The functionalization also influences the conduction mechanism of CQDs. As shown in the right inset of Fig. 10(a), the current at a constant voltage of V = 5 V has a non-monotonic behavior with temperature. No single conduction mechanism could be responsible for this behavior. In fact, as pointed out also in [28], the simultaneous presence of different transport mechanisms in a single device is favored by non-uniform current distributions inside devices containing inorganic/organic nanocomposites. Unlike in bare CQDs, the apparent suppression of the current for |V| < 2.5 V in Fig. 7(b), for all sweeping rates, suggests a Coulomb blockade transport mechanism with a threshold V
th = 2.5 V. The discrepancy between the observed and the estimated threshold voltage for an array of disordered and passivated CQDs with a radius of 2.75 nm and an estimated distance between CQDs of 3 nm, which is 0.38 V [23], could be explained by the Middleton–Wingreen model [29], which states that the threshold voltage in a nanodot array is determined by the size of the array. On the other hand, the Coulomb blockade mechanism is not acting alone since the current is not vanishing below V
th . The almost linear dependence in Fig. 10(b) for all temperatures suggests the presence of the Richardson–Schottky transport mechanism [30] in the CQD–PEG1500N composite (see, for example [31] for other systems in which this transport mechanism is present). This thermionic emission mechanism, which explains also the low current values below room temperature, is described by the formula I∝T 2 exp[−(E
a −bV 1/2)/k
B
T], with b a constant. The activation energies for this mechanism for a constant applied voltage have no uniform dependence on temperature, as can be seen from Fig. 10(b).

Above room temperature, the transient currents at applied step voltages could be fitted also with a biexponential function, the charging and discharging relaxation times at different temperatures being given in Table 3 . Except for the smallest charging time, which increases steadily with temperature, all other relaxation times have non-uniform temperature dependences. Similar complex behaviors with temperature of trapping and detrapping rates as well as of the trap state distribution have been evidenced in [32], for example.

We have demonstrated that bare CQDs as well as CQD–PEG1500N composites show a significant resistance hysteresis, which is stable at a large number of cycles. This stability is due to thermal treatments, which remove the shallow trapping states. This memory effect in both cases is quite significant taking into account the dimensions of the device and is determined by charge trapping states associated with the CQD. Moreover, the CQD–PEG1500N composites show memory effects at high temperatures since the composite can be regarded as a much stable core–shell structure. The transport mechanisms in bare and functionalized CQDs were studied and found to be different. The results suggest that CQDs could be used in memory devices around room temperature, whereas functionalized CQDs could be used in devices with enhanced functionality and working at higher temperatures, which can act as memory devices and/or sensors. The observation of repeatability of the memory effect after a large number of cycles and after large intervals of time is the first step towards fabricating reliable memory devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Romanian National Authority for Scientific Research, CNCS-UEFISCDI, project number PNII-ID-PCCE-2011-2-0069. Iulia Mihalache acknowledges the support of the Sectorial Operational Programme Human Resources Development, project number POSDRU/159/1.5/S/137750.
